href="https://substack.com/redirect/8d1b1374-f4b0-4494-97a4-9a77c51e15c1?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="text-decoration: underline;">piece</a><span> on the CEO of Anthropic, Dario Amodei</span></figcaption></figure></div><div style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="margin: 32px 0;padding: 0;height: 1px;background: rgb(0,0,0,.1);border: none;"></div><h1 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 2em;">Act III. The Absence of the Human</h1><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">And yet, on this whole stage, there is but one reality: none of it truly matters.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>OpenAI may well be glad to shed its freeloaders — the free user base was nearly impossible to monetize. But even beyond such speculation: most consumer AI subscriptions are massively subsidized — by venture capital, by Big Tech, by one of the largest corporate subsidization campaigns in the history of technology. Even the most expensive Claude Max Subscription </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/b11e6f92-cc26-4b3a-8ec5-5f8bcce0f1c6?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">may consume</a><span> up to $5.000 worth of compute.   </span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It has never been about short-term profit, as none of these companies can cover their astronomical computing costs even remotely. The true goal is infrastructure capture. They want to be the default platform—meaning their models become the foundational layer that every other company, developer, and government agency is forced to build upon. They are giving away billions in subsidized compute today to ensure they own the monopolies of tomorrow. This is the field on which the entire theatre takes place.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In this play, there can only be one winner: technology itself. And the principal loser is conspicuous by absence: The Human. In no act of this spectacle did The Human ever appear as subject. The Pentagon was only about securing its perceived dominance. OpenAI fought merely to survive its debt web.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>And Anthropic? The ethical AI company — which assembled its training data from the </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/d36db786-11a0-4f86-ba24-68ac990053b3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">intellectual property of the world</a><span>, while only a privileged few creators can afford the legal battle against such a giant. Before the Pentagon drama, Anthropic had already quietly </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/14248f4a-561f-40ac-b9a5-eeddc3d55cfb?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">lowered the safety requirements</a><span> for its next generation of models.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>And the red lines? The recent mass killing of at least 160 schoolgirls during the strike on Iran was probably </span><a href="https://substack.com/redirect/53986821-3378-4bb1-bab0-e5ea1a14e33a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg" rel="" style="color: rgb(54,55,55);text-decoration: underline;">caused by a faulty target identification</a><span> by Claude’s models. This happened one day after Amodei had been celebrated as the ethical conscience of the AI industry.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>Anthropic is far closer to OpenAI in spirit than either would ever admit. They need each other. One is the villain whose presence lets the other’s virtue shine. “</span><em>Thank God there’s only one Sam Altman!</em><span>” critics may say — while both companies jointly industrialize what Günther Anders once diagnosed as the ground condition of our epoch: the obsolescence of the human in the face of its own creations.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">If all of this is theatre, if the bailout is no bailout, then the play functions not as an attack on the human, which would presuppose intent, but as something staged past the human entirely, in complete structural indifference. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The consumer’s decision to switch to Anthropic changes nothing about the underlying dynamic, even though it feels like resistance. The hand still reaches. What has been replaced is everything behind it.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The true achievement of this spectacle: it takes the genuine moral outrage of its users, their still-functioning ethical intuition, and converts it into a new subscription. The Industrialization of Hype. In this apparatus, Anthropic can never be seen as the antidote. It is merely other side of the same mechanism. What it produces is the ultimate obsolescence: our deepest moral convictions, reduced to raw material for the expansion of the very system they were meant to oppose.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In the end, the entire spectacle of AI ethics revealed itself as what it was from the start: a mechanism for securing backdoor bailouts — and for accelerating the very machinery that no longer requires the human it was supposedly about.</p><p class="button-wrapper" message.plain_text_body:
View this post on the web at https://msukhareva.substack.com/p/the-bailout-that-isnt-a-bailout

This article is a collaboration with Tony Siewert [ https://substack.com/redirect/63b0539a-7766-4fb3-9c59-36324d80056a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], a tech professional who dissects the cultural and philosophical impacts of modern AI. Tony champions the messy, vital human element in the age of automation and explores why, in today's tech landscape, he takes pride in being branded as 'The Antiquated One [ https://substack.com/@tonysiewert1 ]’.
Act I. The Three Actors
In the beginning of March 2026, one and a half million people switched AI providers out of moral outrage. They cancelled their subscriptions to OpenAI and signed up with Anthropic. By every available metric, it was an act of resistance. By every structural measure, it was the exact opposite.  
The stage was being built weeks and months earlier. The cast included Anthropic, OpenAI, and the Pentagon itself. On the surface, the play is about the question of Ethics: can a private company defend its safety protocols against the will of the state? The real drama, however, played out between the lines.
The first company to deploy its models [ https://substack.com/redirect/bee85137-1969-4354-ba4b-d0c298f0e371?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] on the classified networks of the US government was Anthropic, our actor number one. Their Claude Models were already running inside mission-critical intelligence operations [ https://substack.com/redirect/e97655f3-6f11-484a-9f4e-b6831cce8681?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. They even explicitly opened its models for missile defense and combat logistics. 
Anthropic always does everything “correctly” — almost suspiciously so.
Sounds great? We aren’t done yet. Anthropic had a product that built a genuine community. Claude Code became the primary driver [ https://substack.com/redirect/52e47849-a36c-474e-9de2-d8b690daac6e?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of growth velocity and valuation at Anthropic. The latest models — Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 — were leading the major benchmarks in AI, LMSYS Arena [ https://substack.com/redirect/e010ec3d-71f3-4ff5-92d0-495faafa9f5a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] among them.
That is the definition of Aura Farming, but even that wasn’t enough. When literally dealing with the Ministry of War, Anthropic managed to maintain two red lines: 1) Claude would not be used for mass surveillance of American citizens [ https://substack.com/redirect/4980675b-526c-40e3-8945-5c2cf9725236?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. 2) And it would not control fully autonomous lethal weapon systems. The first line was unambiguously ethical. The second existed for a slightly simpler reason: Dario Amodei genuinely did not believe current AI models were ready for that use case [ https://substack.com/redirect/88286781-af49-471f-b279-b9e2f408b5f3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]. 
One might expect that doing everything “correctly” earns a reward. But Actor number 2 has a mind of his own: Enter the Pentagon. Following the general ultra-conservative rollback, the Pentagon was already unhappy about “woke” AI [ https://substack.com/redirect/f2078937-b21f-4c19-ac39-81206eb968f8?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] in January 2026. Weeks of tense negotiations over a $200 million contract followed. On February 24th, Defense Secretary Peter Hegseth delivered an ultimatum to Amodei in person: drop all AI safety rules by Friday, February 27 — or be designated a “supply-chain risk to national security”.
No American company had ever been threatened with such treatment — it is normally reserved for cases like Huawei, a subsidiary of a geopolitical rival. The threat works not through legal precision but through the ambiguity it creates. Though the designation formally applied only to direct military contracts, defense contractors and suppliers are heavily incentivized to drop Anthropic’s products preemptively, if only to avoid the associated stigma. For a company whose models are already deeply embedded in these infrastructures, the designation alone can be existential.
Anthropic — the company that does everything “correctly” — refused to comply and challenged the designation in court. Trump himself weighed in with a post branding Anthropic a “leftwing nutjob” and worse. Anthropic stood alone.
The post [ https://substack.com/redirect/38531cc7-6420-42c0-97c0-bcb3a5e2ba86?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of Donald Trump on Anthropic
Act II. The Bailout That Isn’t a Bailout
Anyone who thinks Anthropic brought this upon itself has forgotten the third player: OpenAI with its CEO Sam Altman.
Unlike Anthropic, OpenAI is in serious trouble. The company that launched the LLM hype with the massive success of ChatGPT has become the central node in a web of circular financing deals that artificially prop up large parts [ https://substack.com/redirect/d5ca7987-62ca-47e7-92cd-44d501506b18?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] of the US tech economy. When Microsoft “invests” in OpenAI only for that money to flow directly back as payment for Azure services, what emerges can hardly be described as value chain as it mimicks a carousel. That such constructions blur the line between creative accounting and antitrust evasion is no secret [ https://substack.com/redirect/06c1f260-657f-49de-aebb-5152f1390a1f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ].
And as tech critic Ed Zitron recently revealed through leaked financial documents, the subsequent revenue numbers that OpenAI has shown to the world are failing to convince anyone that the ‘math is mathing [ https://substack.com/redirect/28a86548-9cda-463c-9f81-53c7c73c3bac?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]’.
The deals have grown so vast that OpenAI is already being labeled [ https://substack.com/redirect/22817f9c-44d6-4813-bdd6-de2977b92039?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] “too big to fail” — and the question of a state-guaranteed bailout hangs openly in the air. Set against Anthropic’s success — better products, no absurd debt mountains, everything done “correctly” — OpenAI’s position turns precarious.
When customers began defecting en masse to Google and flagship model underperformed, the company declared “Code Red” in December 2025 — halting most side projects overnight in a desperate pivot to salvage its core technology.
No AI drama of this era unfolds without OpenAI — this one least of all. First, Sam Altman publicly backed Amodei’s principled stance — despite having refused to shake his hand at the AI summit in New Delhi just weeks earlier.
Sam Altman refuses to take the hand with Dario Amodei (third and second from the right, respectrively)
Then, immediately after the Pentagon branded Anthropic a supply-chain risk, Altman announced two things. OpenAI would step into the Pentagon contract. And it had secured a historic $110 billion funding round. Both on the same day. The entire Anthropic-Pentagon narrative was instantly subsumed under a single name: Sam Altman.
OpenAI’s sudden alignment with the military becomes legible against this backdrop. In late 2025, the administration had explicitly refused a direct bailout — as there were enough frontier companies, and they were allowed to fail. OpenAI was out of options.
By taking the Pentagon contract OpenAI established a new state-secured monopoly. And that is, essentially, a bailout that isn’t a bailout.
Yet Altman wouldn’t be Altman if Anthropic were simply thrown under the bus. In the end, Anthropic may have been the biggest winner of the entire spectacle. Anthropic’s refusal is being celebrated as a second coming of Apple’s [ https://substack.com/redirect/dc66a589-211c-4758-b6fc-567c1542654f?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] famous decision not to install backdoors on its devices. Downloads surged across [ https://substack.com/redirect/7b2c0410-2dbd-450e-9ef6-143710d992c0?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] every app store. Millions switched from ChatGPT to Claude.
What began as a meme quickly became a measurable exodus. Under the banner of #QuitGPT [ https://substack.com/redirect/eb0b3741-a64d-4da9-bf64-a9d35678b2f3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], over 1.5 million users pledged to boycott OpenAI, driving a 295% spike in ChatGPT uninstalls and propelling Claude to number one on the App Store.
Amodei was declared a hero. The Atlantic even published an article [ https://substack.com/redirect/8d1b1374-f4b0-4494-97a4-9a77c51e15c1?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], directly comparing Dario Amodei with Oppenheimer. 
Act III. The Absence of the Human
And yet, on this whole stage, there is but one reality: none of it truly matters.
OpenAI may well be glad to shed its freeloaders — the free user base was nearly impossible to monetize. But even beyond such speculation: most consumer AI subscriptions are massively subsidized — by venture capital, by Big Tech, by one of the largest corporate subsidization campaigns in the history of technology. Even the most expensive Claude Max Subscription may consume [ https://substack.com/redirect/b11e6f92-cc26-4b3a-8ec5-5f8bcce0f1c6?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] up to $5.000 worth of compute.   
It has never been about short-term profit, as none of these companies can cover their astronomical computing costs even remotely. The true goal is infrastructure capture. They want to be the default platform—meaning their models become the foundational layer that every other company, developer, and government agency is forced to build upon. They are giving away billions in subsidized compute today to ensure they own the monopolies of tomorrow. This is the field on which the entire theatre takes place.
In this play, there can only be one winner: technology itself. And the principal loser is conspicuous by absence: The Human. In no act of this spectacle did The Human ever appear as subject. The Pentagon was only about securing its perceived dominance. OpenAI fought merely to survive its debt web.
And Anthropic? The ethical AI company — which assembled its training data from the intellectual property of the world [ https://substack.com/redirect/d36db786-11a0-4f86-ba24-68ac990053b3?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], while only a privileged few creators can afford the legal battle against such a giant. Before the Pentagon drama, Anthropic had already quietly lowered the safety requirements [ https://substack.com/redirect/14248f4a-561f-40ac-b9a5-eeddc3d55cfb?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] for its next generation of models.
And the red lines? The recent mass killing of at least 160 schoolgirls during the strike on Iran was probably caused by a faulty target identification [ https://substack.com/redirect/53986821-3378-4bb1-bab0-e5ea1a14e33a?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] by Claude’s models. This happened one day after Amodei had been celebrated as the ethical conscience of the AI industry.
Anthropic is far closer to OpenAI in spirit than either would ever admit. They need each other. One is the villain whose presence lets the other’s virtue shine. “Thank God there’s only one Sam Altman!” critics may say — while both companies jointly industrialize what Günther Anders once diagnosed as the ground condition of our epoch: the obsolescence of the human in the face of its own creations.
If all of this is theatre, if the bailout is no bailout, then the play functions not as an attack on the human, which would presuppose intent, but as something staged past the human entirely, in complete structural indifference. 
The consumer’s decision to switch to Anthropic changes nothing about the underlying dynamic, even though it feels like resistance. The hand still reaches. What has been replaced is everything behind it.
The true achievement of this spectacle: it takes the genuine moral outrage of its users, their still-functioning ethical intuition, and converts it into a new subscription. The Industrialization of Hype. In this apparatus, Anthropic can never be seen as the antidote. It is merely other side of the same mechanism. What it produces is the ultimate obsolescence: our deepest moral convictions, reduced to raw material for the expansion of the very system they were meant to oppose.
In the end, the entire spectacle of AI ethics revealed itself as what it was from the start: a mechanism for securing backdoor bailouts — and for accelerating the very machinery that no longer requires the human it was supposedly about.

Unsubscribe https://substack..tfkPcA37S8gfUWXa5n7jl82sYHjrhwPENsQ_Jk9kVSo?
message.snippet: A Play in three acts. On the Anthropic-Pentagon Spectacle and what it is costing The Human ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏

What is Reinforcement learning?</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>Let’s start with the definition of Reinforcement Learning (</span><strong>RL</strong><span>).</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">RL is a type of machine learning (besides Supervised and Unsupervised learning) that deals with an entity called an ‘Agent’, trying to learn to perform a task better in its ‘Environment’ through trial and error.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">For example, a deer (Agent) foraging in a forest (Environment) to survive, avoiding being eaten by its predators.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">We will soon move to more AI-related examples, I promise, but first, let’s better understand what the terms ‘Agent’ and ‘Environment’ Agent</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">An agent is the central entity in RL.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It is one that studies and interacts with its environment, makes decisions, takes actions, and learns from its outcomes.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In our previous case, we used a deer as an agent example, but from now on, we will discuss concepts assuming an LLM as the agent as well.</p><div class="captioned-image-container-static" style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;margin: 32px auto;"><figure style="width: 100%;margin: 0 auto;"><table class="image-wrapper" 1.375em;"><strong>3. Environment</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The environment is everything outside the agent with which it interacts.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The function of an environment is to:</p><ul style="margin-top: 0;padding: 0;"><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">Get affected by an agent’s actions</p></li><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">Change its state (or maybe hold its state constant) depending on the agent’s actions</p></li><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">Give the agent a reward or punishment based on its actions, so that the agent can modify its intent the next time it takes an action</p></li></ul><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In the first case, the deer was functioning in a forest as its environment. Based on the deer’s actions (moving around in different directions), either the forest (environment) lets it progress towards food (reward) or it exposes it to a cheetah (punishment).</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In the case of an LLM as an agent, everything outside it, such as the following, is part of its environment:</p><ul style="margin-top: 0;padding: 0;"><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">User inputs</p></li><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">System prompt</p></li><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style="color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;margin-bottom: 0;box-sizing: border-box;padding-left: 4px;font-size: 16px;margin: 0;">Tools / APIs it can call</p></li><li style="margin: 8px 0 0 32px;mso-special-format: bullet;"><p 1.375em;"><strong>4. State</strong></h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">A State is a snapshot of an environment at a time. It is everything an agent sees at a given moment that it can use to make its next decision.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">For a deer agent, it could be its current location in the forest, nearby predators, and the time of day.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">For an LLM agent, it is all the context it has access to at a moment, which it can use to decide its next action.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>But what’s message.plain_text_body:
View this post on the web at https://newsletter.systemdesign.one/p/what-is-reinforcement-learning

Get my system design playbook for FREE on newsletter signup:
Share this post [ https://substack.com/redirect/515c83f0-7c60-4852-8957-042a72e3cc12?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] & I'll send you some rewards for the referrals.
Reinforcement learning (RL) is making a comeback and becoming mainstream.
From humanoid robots and game-playing AIs that beat world champions to the LLMs you chat with every day, all are trained using RL, which lets them learn from experience and get better through feedback.
Contrary to many tutorials that can make it look so hard, RL is actually pretty intuitive, and you really don’t need a PhD to understand how it works.
In this newsletter, we will go through 21 key RL concepts to build a solid foundation from the ground up. These terms are all explained in plain language without using any fancy math equations.
Let’s start from the very beginning…
Want to reduce AI coding bugs before they hit production? (Partner) [ https://substack.com/redirect/49f517d1-ddc3-4bbb-bb74-95efb6358c92?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
AI is writing more production code than ever, but it also introduces more risk.
CodeRabbit’s research found that AI-generated code contains 1.7x more issues, including 75% more logic errors.
That’s exactly why you should grab a copy of the Ultimate Prompting Cheat Sheet [ https://substack.com/redirect/49f517d1-ddc3-4bbb-bb74-95efb6358c92?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ].
Here’s what you get:
Proven prompting techniques to reduce AI coding errors.
Actionable workflows for AI-assisted development.
Clear frameworks for safer AI-generated code.
Practical examples you can apply immediately.
And many more!
These techniques reduce defects without slowing velocity, regardless of which AI coding tool you use.
Sign up and get access to the ultimate prompting cheat sheet right now:
(Thanks to CodeRabbit for partnering on this post & sharing the ultimate prompting cheat sheet [ https://substack.com/redirect/49f517d1-ddc3-4bbb-bb74-95efb6358c92?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ].)
I want to reintroduce Ashish Bamania [ https://substack.com/redirect/f2523ca5-6df0-48b5-97bb-20ddd53bddcd?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] as a guest author.
He’s a self-taught software engineer and an emergency physician. He is also the editor and primary author of the newsletters Into AI [ https://substack.com/redirect/4f4b03bb-a8d1-4905-9d90-60de3a83967b?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ] and Into Quantum [ https://substack.com/redirect/56313cc2-2fa2-4104-a0a5-448941d44c91?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ].
The images used in this newsletter come from his books:
LLMs In 100 Images [ https://substack.com/redirect/0fd1b533-df5b-47a0-a8f9-8c32283c0a2b?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ]
Reinforcement Learning in 100 Images (upcoming)
Grab the book at 20% discount today with the discount code NEO20 [ https://substack.com/redirect/0fd1b533-df5b-47a0-a8f9-8c32283c0a2b?j=eyJ1IjoiM28yYngifQ.lK_fWvTbokdbQgO91mTS9lmdiqT7y_r7zXBY_yDFDTg ], pre-applied to the link above!
1. What is Reinforcement learning?
Let’s start with the definition of Reinforcement Learning (RL).
RL is a type of machine learning (besides Supervised and Unsupervised learning) that deals with an entity called an ‘Agent’, trying to learn to perform a task better in its ‘Environment’ through trial and error.
For example, a deer (Agent) foraging in a forest (Environment) to survive, avoiding being eaten by its predators.
We will soon move to more AI-related examples, I promise, but first, let’s better understand what the terms ‘Agent’ and ‘Environment’ mean.
2. Agent
An agent is the central entity in RL.
It is one that studies and interacts with its environment, makes decisions, takes actions, and learns from its outcomes.
In our previous case, we used a deer as an agent example, but from now on, we will discuss concepts assuming an LLM as the agent as well.
3. Environment
The environment is everything outside the agent with which it interacts.
The function of an environment is to:
Get affected by an agent’s actions
Change its state (or maybe hold its state constant) depending on the agent’s actions
Give the agent a reward or punishment based on its actions, so that the agent can modify its intent the next time it takes an action
In the first case, the deer was functioning in a forest as its environment. Based on the deer’s actions (moving around in different directions), either the forest (environment) lets it progress towards food (reward) or it exposes it to a cheetah (punishment).
In the case of an LLM as an agent, everything outside it, such as the following, is part of its environment:
User inputs
System prompt
Tools / APIs it can call
System responses (tool results, API outputs, error messages)
Context (documents, conversation history, files)
4. State
A State is a snapshot of an environment at a time. It is everything an agent sees at a given moment that it can use to make its next decision.
For a deer agent, it could be its current location in the forest, nearby predators, and the time of day.
For an LLM agent, it is all the context it has access to at a moment, which it can use to decide its next action.
But what’s action?
5. Action...

March 14th 2026

The Economist This Weekend

 
Illustration: Wanjin Kim

Robert Guest

Deputy editor

My children are now old enough to read The Economist. So they notice when I recycle lines I have previously used on them, such as this one from a story in 2022: “Trying to learn about sex from Hollywood is like watching James Bond for tips on a career as a British civil servant.”

It may seem that sex is everywhere in modern culture, but frank, realistic discussion of it is still too rare. And this matters because sexual dysfunction is widespread. It makes people miserable, wrecks marriages and is frequently a predictor of other health problems, such as diabetes. It is often cheap and simple to treat, yet remains untreated because people—even doctors—are embarrassed to talk about it. So here’s a selection of evidence-based writing about one of life’s greatest sources of joy and angst. It includes a story I wrote about data, and why researchers know surprisingly little about a subject that so many people spend so much time thinking about.

New Event Blast

There's a new blast for Gemini 3 Paris Hackathon:

Lunch is served. ☀️
Step away from the screens for a moment, refuel, and come back ready to crush those bugs!
Bon appétit!

View Event https://cerebralvalley.ai/e/gemini-3-paris-hackathon?utm_source=event-blast&utm_medium=email

----------------------------------------

Gemini 3 Paris Hackathon

Dates

Saturday Mar 14 9:00 AM - Saturday Mar 14 10:00 PM (CET)



Add to your calendar:

Google Calendar https://calendar.google.com/calendar/render?action=TEMPLATE&text=Gemini%203%20Paris%20Hackathon&dates=20260314T090000%2F20260314T220000&ctz=Europe%2FBelgrade

Outlook Calendar https://outlook.live.com/calendar/0/deeplink/compose?path=%2Fcalendar%2Faction%2Fcompose&rru=addevent&subject=Gemini%203%20Paris%20Hackathon&startdt=2026-03-14T08%3A00%3A00.000Z&enddt=2026-03-14T21%3A00%3A00.000Z

----------------------------------------

Want to unsubscribe? Edit your notification settings here https://cerebralvalley.ai/profile?open=true&tab=notifications

cerebralvalley.ai https://cerebralvalley.ai/

16px;line-height: 26px;"><div class="body markup" dir="auto" style="text-align: initial;font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;width: 100%;word-break: break-word;margin-bottom: 16px;font-family: 'SF Pro Display', -apple-system, system-ui, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Inter', 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, 'Apple Color Emoji', 'Segoe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: 400;"><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;margin-top: 0;">What is time? The answer may seem obvious, but there is a fundamental distinction that exists between “manifest time” — our psychological, subjective experience — and physical time, which is embedded within the laws of physics. But our  sense of time may be incredibly misleading. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In this interview, theoretical physicist Jim Al-Khalili explores the mysteries of time, from the Big Bang and the “block universe” model to time travel and the famous “grandfather paradox.”</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">He breaks the “problem of time” into four key questions: Does time actually flow? How can quantum field theory be reconciled with general relativity? What defines the present moment? And why does time seem to move in only one direction?</p><p class="button-wrapper" UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.625em;">Transcript</h2><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><em>The below is a true verbatim transcript taken directly from the video. It captures the conversation exactly as it happened.</em></p><div style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="margin: 32px 0;padding: 0;height: 1px;background: rgb(0,0,0,.1);border: none;"></div><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>My name is Jim Al-Khalili and I’m Emeritus Professor of Physics at the University of Surrey. My book is called </span><em>On Time</em><span>: </span><em>The Physics That Makes the Universe Tick</em><span>. If you look up the problem of time online, there will be various definitions of what is regarded as the problem of time.</span></p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">The problem of time</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">I like to lay out four different distinct problems. One is whether time flows. The second, how can we reconcile quantum field theory with Einstein’s general theory of relativity? Third, what is special about now?</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The fourth and final problem of time: where does this direction of time come from?</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">At the end of this interview, hopefully we’ll lay out the problems and the questions and the paradoxes, some of which we’ve figured out, others remain.</p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Chapter 1: Does time flow?</h3><h4 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.125em;">Physical time vs manifest time</h4><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">For me as a physicist, I try to understand the external world, try and make sense of it. And to do that objectively, we have to extract ourselves from the thing that we’re studying. When it comes to time, there’s a problem because we are unavoidably embedded within time. We can’t extract ourselves from it and view it objectively.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Very often, physicists and philosophers who study the nature of time make this distinction between physical time that is embedded within the laws of physics and our own perception of time, psychological time.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>I like to use the term </span><em>manifest time</em><span>. I think it was first used by the philosopher Craig Callender. Manifest time is a way of compartmentalizing our perception of time from time that is external to us.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">One of the most powerful distinctions between our perception of time, manifest time, and physical time, time that appears in our laws of physics external to our senses, is this notion that we feel very strongly that time is flowing. There’s some continuum. There’s change. We tend to think that time speeds up as we get older.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Where did time go? Suddenly, I’m — the last 10, 20 years, my kids have grown up. How did that happen? And yet your childhood seemed to stretch on forever.</p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Why time feels faster as we age</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">There’s a theory that suggests it’s to do with the laying down of new experiences.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It’s true that one year when you’re five years old, the period between one birthday and the next is a really long time. One year when you’re 50 years old goes by in a flash.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">There’s the opposite property to time when you look at shorter intervals. Rather than the laying down of new experiences and being active slowing time down, compare half an hour sitting in the dentist’s waiting room when you have nothing to do — you haven’t got your phone on, you’re not reading anything — with half an hour at a party where you’re enjoying yourself.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">At the party, you’re laying down your experiences. You’re meeting people, you’re chatting to them, you have a lot of input coming into your senses. And yet that half hour at the party goes by much more quickly than the time that drags when you’re sitting in the dentist’s waiting room.</p><div style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="margin: 32px 0;padding: 0;height: 1px;background: rgb(0,0,0,.1);border: none;"></div><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Time and change in philosophy and physics </h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">A concept closely related to the nature of time is the idea of change. When something changes, we think of it in a common sense way as changing over time, as time flowing and something changes.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">This idea goes all the way back to the ancient Greeks who couldn’t agree on whether time was fundamental, that it had to exist and then change happens within it, or whether change is the fundamental concept and time simply reflects that something changes.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Physicists have argued about this and they argue about it to this day. The great physicist Richard Feynman said that “time is what happens when nothing else happens.” That somehow time exists in and of itself.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Isaac Newton had the view that time was external to the universe, external certainly to space and things that happen within space.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">That there’s this cosmic clock that inexorably ticks by the seconds, the minutes, the hours, the years regardless of what we are doing. You know, we can’t stop that time. We’re just caught in the flow and time keeps on going. So that absolute notion of time is something that Isaac Newton explained and something that, in everyday life, I guess, is the common sense view of time. There is this external clock that’s ticking by regardless of whether we think time is speeding up or slowing down. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Einstein and the end of absolute time</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Of course, that idea of an absolute external time was thrown away entirely when Einstein developed his theories of relativity.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Einstein explained there is no such thing as absolute time. Time is relative. And yet we still have this strong psychological feeling that there’s a past, there’s a future, there’s a now, there’s a flow.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Our consciousness is moving along this river of time, gobbling up the future, spitting out the past. The present moment is ever changing. So that notion of change is embedded within this idea that time’s flowing. And yet, in the laws of physics, there is change, but there is no flow in and of itself to time. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Time in the equations of physics</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;"><span>In most of our equations of physics that describe how something, a system changes, — yes it changes over time, but time comes in in a very almost trivial way. As a humble coordinate, a number, a parameter, a symbol, lowercase </span><em>t</em><span> that sits inside your equation. It’s not changing.</span></p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">If you have an equation that describes a system, you can look at the math — look at the equation. What does it mean when we evolve this equation in time?</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It just means we’re solving this equation for a value of this coordinate. What a system is doing now is described by what it’s doing at this particular value of coordinate time.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">We can also evolve it forward to see what it’s doing in the future, or evolve it backwards to see what it’s doing in the past. But there’s no flow.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Time is simply a number that appears in the equations. And there must be more to time that. Or certainly that’s our psychological feeling about time — that there is change and we experience this change as a continuum, but where is that change? Where is that flow in the equation of physics? Doesn’t exist. </p><div style="font-size: 16px;line-height: 26px;"><hr style="margin: 32px 0;padding: 0;height: 1px;background: rgb(0,0,0,.1);border: none;"></div><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Chapter 2: How do we reconcile quantum field theory with the general theory of relativity? </h3><h4 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.125em;">Special relativity and time dilation</h4><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It seems quite natural to us that time can speed up or slow down depending on what we’re doing. If we’re having fun, time goes by quickly, if you’re bored, time drags. But we know that’s just in our subjective experience. It’s not what really happens to time. Surely, external to our experience, time continues at a constant rate everywhere in the universe. Well, of course, that’s Newtonian time, and that’s the time of our everyday experience.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">But Einstein comes along and says, “Actually, the idea that time speeds up, slows down isn’t just subjective, isn’t just in our heads. There is a real physical phenomenon whereby time can run at different rates for different observers.” What we say in different frames of reference. That was a revolution in physics.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Einstein, of course, had two theories of relativity. The first one in 1905 was forced upon him by not studying the nature of time and space, initially, but by studying the nature of light and the speed of light. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Physicist the late 19th century had been searching for the medium that carries light. Now, light was known to be a wave. Light, therefore, physicists believed, needed something to travel through some medium, this ether. The fact that I couldn’t find it was very puzzling, and Einstein overthrew the whole concept of what it means, what time and space means, by suggesting that light doesn’t need an ether at all. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Which means that however fast you’re moving relative to someone else, you should still see light moving at the same speed. What does this mean? If I shine a torch out into space, and then a friend jumps in a rocket and flies, let’s say, at three quarters of the light speed. So I will see light overtaking them in their spacecraft at a quarter light speed. Einstein says, “You would expect that they would see light overtaking them at that same speed.” But no, they would still see that light overtaking them at the same speed that you see it leaving you. All observers, however fast they’re moving relative to each other, will see light moving at the same speed. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It shouldn’t make any sense at all, until you realize that they must have different concepts of space and time. And in fact, my friend traveling in the spacecraft trying to catch up with this light beam, if you were to look at their clock on board their spacecraft, you’d see it ticking by more slowly. That’s the sun’s workout. You change the nature of space and time in order to preserve the constancy of the speed of light for everyone.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">So Einstein came to this conclusion in his special theory of relativity due to the constancy of the speed of light and the fact that all motion is relative. So no one can say, “I am definitely standing still and you’re moving.” To come up with the idea that time and space get altered. And since we’re talking here about the nature of time, this is what leads to the idea of time dilation. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">That time can be seen to run slower on a moving clock as it moves past you. Now, if someone’s moving past me close to the speed of light, I will see them doing everything in slow motion. It’s just not just that their clock is moving slower. Somehow their high speed is messing with the mechanics of the clock. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">That whole frame of reference I will see time running slower in it. However, since all motion is relative, the person board the spacecraft that I see behaving in slow motion would see me behaving in slow motion because for them, I’m doing all the moving. And so it’s my time that is running slower.</p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Evidence for time dilation: Muons</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">This idea of time dilation seems like just a thought experiment or some notion that Einstein’s come up with surely doesn’t happen in the real world. Well, it does. For example, muons are subatomic particles that are created in the upper atmosphere. Cosmic rays hit the molecules of air in the upper atmosphere and the energy from the collision creates a whole stream of particles. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Among them is this particle called the muon, which is like the more massive cousin of the electron. Muons aren’t like electrons in the sense that they’re not stable. They only live for a fraction of a second. These muons that are created in the upper atmosphere then stream down to earth. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, we can measure how fast they’re moving close to the speed of light. Nevertheless, for the distance they have to cover at that speed, they would have to live a lot longer than their normal lifetime, the lifetime they would have at rest. But of course, they do manage to get down to the ground and we can capture muons in our detectors so we know they’ve arrived from the upper atmosphere. How do they do this? Well, because they’re going close to the speed of light, their clocks, their internal clocks that tick by their lifetime slow down. So they are living their life in slow motion, which gives them enough time to reach the earth. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, the counter explanation to this is how does the muon see things? Surely, if all motion is relative, the muon sees our clocks running slower. Well, of course, from the muon’s perspective, something else happens when you go close to the speed of light, namely distances shrink. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">So along with what’s called time dilation, the slowing down of time, there’s length contraction. And the length contraction is the distance the muon has to travel from the upper atmosphere to sea level. The muon is moving so close to the speed of light that it sees this distance shrunk. And it says, “Well, I’m only living for two microseconds. I’m counting how long I’m living for. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The reason I can get down to the ground is because I don’t have to travel that far.” So everything fits and works logically. But this idea that time slows down is real. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Gravity slows time: General Relativity</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">This notion of time dilation is part of Einstein’s special theory of relativity. There’s another way that time slows down. And that is part of his general theory of relativity, which he developed a decade later. And that’s where we have to bring gravity into the story. You see, in special relativity, it’s all about things moving at constant velocities. No acceleration, no speeding up, slowing down, going around in circles. Things have to move in a straight line at a constant speed. And then you can use the rules of special relativity to talk about time intervals and lengths and how fast clocks tick and so on. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Once Einstein wanted to generalize that, he realized he had to bring in acceleration. What he referred to as the happiest thought of his life was something we now teach as the principle of equivalence, which is to say that acceleration is equivalent to gravity.</p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, we’re sort of aware of this in everyday language because when a car or an aircraft is accelerating, we talk about the G-force. Now, that G is G for gravity. When you’re pushed back in your seat as your car accelerates, that’s exactly the same force as you feel when you’re laying down with your head resting on the pillow, if you’re accelerating at 1G. This principle of equivalence led to the idea that in order to explain the structure and nature of four-dimensional space-time, one has to come up with a different picture of gravity, since gravity is linked to acceleration. And that led to Einstein’s general theory of relativity, which was a theory of gravity that replaced, yet again he’s replacing something that Newton has given us. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In general relativity, Einstein gives us a new picture of gravity. In that intervening 10-year period between special theory in 1905 and general relativity in 1915, Einstein and other physicists realize that time is the fourth dimension, and it’s actually you have to talk about it in terms of four-dimensional space-time. His general theory says, “Yes, time is the fourth dimension, part of space-time, but gravity curves space-time. Gravity changes the shape of space-time.” </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, we can’t perceive space-time as a four-dimensional whole. We can only have instruments that measure distances in space or clocks that measure intervals in time, so we have to sort of separate the two. So how does that look to us? Well, time gets slowed down by gravity. And again, this is not an optical illusion. This is in a sense more fascinating, more fun than the time-dilation of special relativity, because special relativity, you only see that effect when you’re going close to the speed of light. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">When I explain this, for example, when I give school’s talks, and the kids will ask, “How do you know you’re just making this up?” So, well, everyone of you that uses a smartphone, you use GPS, you use Google Maps, you are making use of the fact that time is running at different rates, depending on how strong gravity is. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">You see, the closer you are to the surface of the Earth, the stronger the gravitational field and the slower time runs. The further out you’re going to space, the weaker gravity is, and the faster time can run. It’s only just tiny fractions of a second, but it matters. GPS, the satellites that are sending radio signals down to our phones, they are in orbit around the Earth. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">They’re feeling slightly weaker gravity than the clocks on Earth. And so time is running faster on board those satellites than it is on Earth. So we have to take this into account, in a sense, slow down the clocks on board the satellites so they synchronize with clocks on Earth. Because if we don’t do that, satellites would not be able to locate our position accurately. So it’s a real effect. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It’s true, you know, that my head is aging faster than my feet. But I’m tiny, tiny fractions of a second, but it’s there. And of course it gets much more dramatic if you go and find a much stronger gravitational field. It sounds ridiculous, but actually the physics is all correct. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">We’re used to describing events that are happening at a point in space and a moment in time. So in physics, when you want to define an event, say the snapping of my fingers, it happened at this point in space and at that moment in time, we need four numbers to describe it. We know that. Even Isaac Newton would have understood that. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Space-time and the block universe</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In special relativity, once we unify time and space, time is not just another disconnected number, a place in space and a moment in time. It’s a point in four-dimensional space time. Time is this fourth dimension. Now, saying time is the fourth dimension is easy enough, but what does it actually mean? We can’t visualize four dimensions. We can only visualize three dimensions. Our brains are three-dimensional. There’s a trick we can do here. So I have a three-dimensional construct. What if time replaced the third spatial dimension? Now I have a three-dimensional construct, two of space and one of time, that I can visualize. And this leads to an idea very useful in physics called the block universe. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In a sense, it’s a bit like sheets of paper in a book. So each sheet or slice is all of space at one moment in time. At the one end is time at the earliest moment. The direction is the time axis. And then you have these slices of space for each moment in time. Now, we experience time as going by continuously, but you can imagine there’s a slice for every moment in time. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The idea of a block universe is very useful because we can now draw a picture of it and think about space time having thrown away one of the dimensions of space. What does it then mean for us to be moving through space time? While here, physicists use the idea of a world line. So this is a line that moves through this block universe. It can start at a particular point, which coincides to a point in space, and a moment in time, and ends with another point in space at a later moment in time. Every one of us has a world line. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">My world line began when I was born. I mean, let’s make an approximate point about that being the moment of my birth, being the beginning of my world line. And it will end when I die. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">That world line isn’t a straight line. It’s wavy because although I’m moving through time, I’m also moving through space. Now, if I were to spend my whole life without moving, then I would have a straight line, world line. But actually, it waves as I move through space while traveling through time. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">And physicists use world lines very usefully when it comes to describing things like particles colliding with each other and how they move and the speed that they’re moving at. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Does time really exist?</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Probably the most ambitious project in physics over the past, certainly half a century or more, has been to reconcile quantum mechanics, or more correctly, one should say quantum field theory, which is the more modern reincarnation of the quantum mechanics of 100 years ago, quantum field theory with Einstein’s general theory of relativity, namely, the theory of the very small that has time as a coordinate and the theory that describes the structure of space-time itself. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">One early attempt at doing this led to what we call the Wheeler-DeWitt equation. Wheeler, John Wheeler and DeWitt, were two cosmologists who try to come up with a way of unifying quantum mechanics and relativity theory and devise this equation, which has a very remarkable and strange feature, namely that it has no time in it. It just is. And this is an equation that one can argue describes the state of the entire universe, the quantum state of the universe, and there’s no time in it. So this would then add support to the notion that at a fundamental level, time is an illusion, time doesn’t really exist at all. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, of course, the Wheeler-DeWitt equation describes the universe as a whole, and you can only really appreciate the universe as a whole if you were able to extract yourself from outside it. We only ever view the universe from within. But the Wheeler-DeWitt equation has led to the idea that since we perceive time, it seems very real and other areas of physics, maybe time is a so-called emergent property of reality. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, emergence is a rather complex notion. There’s what you might call weak emergence, which is the idea that, for example, an example of weak emergence is the wetness of water. You can’t appreciate the wetness of water just by examining one H2O molecule. You need trillions and trillions of water molecules to come together, and the notion of wetness appears. Or temperature is another example. There is no such thing as temperature when you’re just looking at one or two molecules bouncing around and bumping into each other. But zoom out and look at a box of gas molecules bumping into each other, and suddenly you get this notion of temperature that you can ascribe to it. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">We could, if we studied the microscopic structure of a system enough, determine, deduce that once you scale it up, you will see properties like temperature or the wetness of water emerging. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Then there’s a notion of strong emergence, which is, so consciousness is a good example of that, that you could never hit upon the idea of consciousness just by looking at the interaction of individual neurons in the brain. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">So, emergence is something that appears, grows out of something more fundamental. And there’s been this argument that time is also an emergent property, it grows out of something deeper, something more fundamental. And the suggestion is that that deeper thing is embedded within the quantum realm. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">So, in quantum, even though in quantum mechanics and the famous equation of quantum mechanics, Schrodinger’s equation, we’re Schrodinger developed this equation 100 years ago, and it describes how the quantum state of a, say, an atom evolves in time. That’s an equation that does have time in it. We teach physicists at university that this Schrodinger equation that depends on time can be tweaked and modified, and you can arrive at a new version of it, which is called the time independent Schrodinger equation, where time doesn’t exist at all. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">The Wheeler-DeWitt equation for describing the whole universe, which brings gravity into quantum mechanics, behaves in a similar way. You’ve extracted time from it entirely. So, how is it then that time emerges from a timeless universe? And that is still a subject that’s being studied and debated and argued about today.</p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">The debate: Eternalism vs presentism</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">In order to then try and have some concrete idea of what time is, philosophers have come up with different ways of describing, you know, whether there was a special present moment that devised the past from the future. Einstein tells us that time is the fourth dimension, and others will argue that time doesn’t exist at all. It’s all just an illusion. It’s a construct. It’s something that we’ve invented, or it’s the thing that clocks measure. So, for example, if we divide time up into three parts, the past, the present, and the future, one might argue that the past has gone. We no longer have access to it. All we have access to is records of the past, but these are records that we don’t access in the present moment. The actual events of the past are gone. They don’t exist. The future has yet to be, even in a deterministic universe where everything is preordained, nevertheless, the future hasn’t happened yet, so it doesn’t exist. All that leaves is the present moment. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">But the present moment isn’t extended. It’s the dividing line between the past and the future. It’s the shadow between the light and the dark. It doesn’t have an existence in and of itself. It’s just the gap between them. So, if the past doesn’t exist, the future doesn’t exist, and the present doesn’t have any extent that it doesn’t exist either, we’ve done away with all of time, then time is an illusion. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, that sounds a bit like sort of a philosophical trickery, and in a sense it is, because there’s the opposite view which is the one that’s given to us by Einstein, which is to say that if time is the fourth dimension, then just like the dimensions of space, all points in space exist and are equally real. If time is also a dimension, then all points in time exist and are equally real. Therefore, rather than the past, present, and future all being an illusion, they are all equally real. They all exist. Time is, in a sense, frozen. We experience a present moment. We are sort of, you can think of it in terms of our consciousness drifting along that time axis, and we’re stuck on that rail, we’re moving along. But the time itself, for all eternity, just is. All times coexist. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">So, this is an idea called eternalism, and I think probably if push comes to shove, most physicists would subscribe to it, because we know that Einstein’s description of time is the best one we have. The idea of eternalism, that all times are equally real in the block universe, can seem quite bleak. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, we already had a notion that reality is rather bleak if we follow what Newton taught us, namely that we might well live in what’s called a deterministic universe, where the future is predetermined, preordained, even though it hasn’t happened yet. That’s what’s called the clockwork universe, where everything is ticking by mechanistically and everything. Causes lead to effects, and if we were powerful enough, we could work out what those effects would be. We could be able, we could predict the future. In reality, we can’t predict the future for a number of reasons. But in Newton’s clockwork universe, even though that future is preordained, it’s destined to be, it still doesn’t exist. And so, we can still imagine that we have free will, free agency. You know, maybe you look back after something has happened, you say, well, it was ever thus that was ever going to be the way it has, because the universe has evolved deterministically. But when it comes to the block universe and eternalism, that future, it’s not that that future has yet to unfold, it’s already there waiting for us. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Somehow that is a more stark description of determinism and a stronger assault on free will. What’s the point of thinking that I’m making free choices if that future is already out there? Now, of course, what saves us is that we are embedded within the universe, within the block universe. If you could pull yourself outside of space-time, the so-called God’s eye view, you could see all times mapped out. You could see exactly past, present, and future all equally real. But we don’t have that privilege, vantage point. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Embedded within the universe and even in a fully deterministic universe, the fact is we can never predict what is going to happen in advance. We are unable to predict this. So you may refer to that as just the illusion of free will, but for me, I think that’s good enough. So I subscribe to a philosophical view called compatibilism, which says that, yes, I have free will even though I live in a deterministic universe. And the reason I say that is because even if the future is preordained and set out in the block universe in this eternalist picture, I’m never able to predict it in advance. So I’m thinking of making that I’m making free choices, and that’s good enough. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Of course, eternalism, the idea that all times past, present, and future all coexist, according to relativity, isn’t the only option available. Philosophers have also argued that maybe an alternative idea is what’s called presentism, which is that only the present moment exists, or past presentism, the past and the present exist, or the growing universe model where the past and present exist but slowly gobble up the future. There’s something called the spotlight theory, the idea that it’s a light shining on one moment that’s changing along the time axis. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">But the problem with all these alternative theories to eternalism, presentism, past presentism, and so on, is that they come into conflict with all our current attempts at coming up with a theory of quantum gravity, whether one subscribes to things like string theory or loop quantum gravity, all these mathematical ideas that unify quantum mechanics of relativity all seem to require the eternalist picture. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">It’s the one that makes most sense when we’re trying to combine quantum mechanics and relativity to solve this problem, the first problem of physical time. And of course, most importantly here is that the eternalist picture says there is nothing special about the present moment. There’s nothing special about now. We experience now subjectively and think it’s special, dividing the past from the future, but in physics there’s nothing special about this moment. It’s just a point on the time axis in four-dimensional spacetime. </p><h3 class="header-anchor-post" style="position: relative;font-family: 'SF Pro Display',-apple-system-headline,system-ui,-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,'Segoe UI',Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif,'Apple Color Emoji','Segoe UI Emoji','Segoe UI Symbol';font-weight: bold;-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased;-webkit-appearance: optimizelegibility;-moz-appearance: optimizelegibility;appearance: optimizelegibility;margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 1.16em;font-size: 1.375em;">Chapter 3: Is there a “now”?</h3><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Even in Einstein’s special theory of relativity, we can appreciate the concept of a now, an absolute universal present moment simply doesn’t make sense. One can imagine two events. I can experience two events that to me seem to have happened at the same time. They’re simultaneous events. </p><p style="margin: 0 0 20px 0;color: rgb(54,55,55);line-height: 26px;font-size: 16px;">Now, if I know they took place at an equal distance from me, then even though it’s taken some fraction of a second for the light from those two events, the signal to reach my eyes, I can work back with and say, “Well, if I saw them happening at the same time, going back, they must have actually like flashes of light.
